mn116:0.2: Sutta Mindfulness Immersed in the Body
mn116:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
mn116:1.3: Now at that time a large number of monks, after the meal, on returning from their alms round, had gathered at the meeting hall when this discussion arose:
mn116:1.4: “Isn’t it amazing, friends! Isn’t it astounding!—
mn116:1.5: the extent to which mindfulness immersed in the body, when developed & pursued, is said by the Blessed One who knows, who sees—the worthy one, rightly self-awakened—to be of great fruit & great benefit.” And this discussion came to no conclusion.
mn116:1.6: Then the Blessed One, emerging from his seclusion in the evening, went to the meeting hall and, on arrival, sat down on a seat made ready.
mn116:2.1: As he was sitting there, he addressed the monks:
mn116:2.2: “For what topic are you gathered together here? And what was the discussion that came to no conclusion?”
mn116:2.3: “Just now, lord, after the meal, on returning from our alms round, we gathered at the meeting hall when this discussion arose:
mn116:2.4: ‘Isn’t it amazing, friends! Isn’t it astounding!—
mn116:2.5: the extent to which mindfulness immersed in the body, when developed & pursued, is said by the Blessed One who knows, who sees—worthy & rightly self-awakened—to be of great fruit & great benefit.’
mn116:2.6: This was the discussion that had come to no conclusion when the Blessed One arrived.”
mn116:2.7: (The Blessed One said:) “And how is mindfulness immersed in the body developed, how is it pursued, so as to be of great fruit & great benefit?
mn116:2.8: “There is the case where a monk—having gone to the wilderness, to the shade of a tree, or to an empty building—sits down folding his legs crosswise, holding his body erect and establishing mindfulness to the fore.
mn116:2.9: Always mindful, he breathes in; mindful he breathes out.
mn116:2.10: Breathing in long, he discerns, ‘I am breathing in long’; or breathing out long, he discerns, ‘I am breathing out long.’
mn116:2.11: Or breathing in short, he discerns, ‘I am breathing in short’; or breathing out short, he discerns, ‘I am breathing out short.’
mn116:2.12: He trains himself, ‘I will breathe in sensitive to the entire body.’ He trains himself, ‘I will breathe out sensitive to the entire body.’
mn116:2.13: He trains himself, ‘I will breathe in calming bodily fabrication.’ He trains himself, ‘I will breathe out calming bodily fabrication.’
mn116:2.14: And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned,
mn116:3.1: and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:3.2: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:3.3: “And further, when walking, the monk discerns, ‘I am walking.’ When standing, he discerns, ‘I am standing.’ When sitting, he discerns, ‘I am sitting.’ When lying down, he discerns, ‘I am lying down.’
mn116:3.4: Or however his body is disposed, that is how he discerns it.
mn116:3.5: And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned,
mn116:3.6: and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:3.7: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:3.8: “And further, when going forward & returning, he makes himself fully alert; when looking toward & looking away… when bending & extending his limbs… when carrying his outer cloak, his upper robe, & his bowl… when eating, drinking, chewing, & savoring… when urinating & defecating… when walking, standing, sitting, falling asleep, waking up, talking, & remaining silent, he makes himself fully alert.
mn116:3.9: And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned,
mn116:3.10: and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:3.11: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:3.12: “And further, the monk reflects on this very body from the soles of the feet on up, from the crown of the head on down, surrounded by skin and full of various kinds of unclean things:
mn116:4.1: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, tendons, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, large intestines, small intestines, gorge, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, skin-oil, saliva, mucus, fluid in the joints, urine.’
mn116:4.2: Just as if a sack with openings at both ends were full of various kinds of grain—
mn116:4.3: wheat, rice, mung beans, kidney beans, sesame seeds, husked rice—
mn116:4.4: and a man with good eyesight, pouring it out, were to reflect,
mn116:4.5: ‘This is wheat. This is rice. These are mung beans. These are kidney beans. These are sesame seeds. This is husked rice’;
mn116:4.6: in the same way, the monk reflects on this very body from the soles of the feet on up, from the crown of the head on down, surrounded by skin and full of various kinds of unclean things:
mn116:4.7: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, tendons, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, large intestines, small intestines, gorge, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, skin-oil, saliva, mucus, fluid in the joints, urine.’
mn116:4.8: And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned,
mn116:4.9: and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:4.10: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:4.11: “And further, the monk contemplates this very body—however it stands, however it is disposed—in terms of properties:
mn116:4.12: ‘In this body there is the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, & the wind property.’
mn116:4.13: Just as a dexterous butcher or his apprentice, having killed a cow, would sit at a crossroads cutting it up into pieces,
mn116:5.1: the monk contemplates this very body—however it stands, however it is disposed—in terms of properties:
mn116:5.2: ‘In this body there is the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, & the wind property.’
mn116:5.3: And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned,
mn116:5.4: and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:5.5: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:5.6: “And further, as if he were to see a corpse cast away in a charnel ground—one day, two days, three days dead—bloated, livid, & festering,
mn116:5.7: he applies it to this very body,
mn116:5.8: ‘This body, too: Such is its nature, such is its future, such its unavoidable fate’…
mn116:6.4: “Or again, as if he were to see a corpse cast away in a charnel ground, picked at by crows, vultures, & hawks, by dogs, hyenas, & various other creatures…
mn116:6.9: a skeleton smeared with flesh & blood, connected with tendons…
mn116:6.10: a fleshless skeleton smeared with blood, connected with tendons…
mn116:6.11: a skeleton without flesh or blood, connected with tendons…
mn116:6.12: bones detached from their tendons, scattered in all directions—here a hand bone, there a foot bone, here a shin bone, there a thigh bone, here a hip bone, there a back bone, here a rib, there a chest bone, here a shoulder bone, there a neck bone, here a jaw bone, there a tooth, here a skull…
mn116:6.18: the bones whitened, somewhat like the color of shells…
mn116:6.19: piled up, more than a year old…
mn116:6.21: He applies it to this very body,
mn116:6.22: ‘This body, too: Such is its nature, such is its future, such its unavoidable fate.’
mn116:6.23: “And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned,
mn116:6.24: and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:6.25: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:6.26: [The Four Jhānas] “And further, quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities, he enters & remains in the first jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of seclusion, accompanied by directed thought & evaluation.
mn116:6.27: He permeates & pervades, suffuses & fills this very body with the rapture & pleasure born of seclusion.
mn116:6.28: Just as if a dexterous bathman or bathman’s apprentice would pour bath powder into a brass basin and knead it together, sprinkling it again & again with water, so that his ball of bath powder—saturated, moisture-laden, permeated within & without—would nevertheless not drip;
mn116:6.29: even so, the monk permeates… this very body with the rapture & pleasure born of seclusion. There is nothing of his entire body unpervaded by rapture & pleasure born of seclusion.
mn116:6.30: And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned,
mn116:6.31: and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:6.32: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:6.33: “Then, with the stilling of directed thoughts & evaluations, he enters & remains in the second jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of concentration, unification of awareness free from directed thought & evaluation—internal assurance.
mn116:6.34: He permeates & pervades, suffuses & fills this very body with the rapture & pleasure born of concentration.
mn116:6.35: Just like a lake with spring-water welling up from within, having no inflow from the east, west, north, or south, and with the skies supplying abundant showers time & again, so that the cool fount of water welling up from within the lake would permeate & pervade, suffuse & fill it with cool waters, there being no part of the lake unpervaded by the cool waters;
mn116:6.36: even so, the monk permeates… this very body with the rapture & pleasure born of concentration. There is nothing of his entire body unpervaded by rapture & pleasure born of concentration.
mn116:7.1: And as he remains thus heedful, ardent, & resolute, any memories & resolves related to the household life are abandoned, and with their abandoning his mind gathers & settles inwardly, grows unified & concentrated.
mn116:7.2: This is how a monk develops mindfulness immersed in the body.
mn116:7.3: “Then, with the fading of rapture, he remains equanimous, mindful, & alert, and senses pleasure with the body. He enters & remains in the third jhāna, of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous & mindful, he has a pleasant abiding.’
mn116:7.4: He permeates & pervades, suffuses & fills this very body with the pleasure divested of rapture.
mn116:7.5: Just as in a lotus pond, some of the lotuses, born & growing in the water, stay immersed in the water and flourish without standing up out of the water, so that they are permeated & pervaded, suffused & filled with cool water from their roots to their tips, and nothing of those lotuses would be unpervaded with cool water;
